content.text: Python Generators & Iterators: A Complete Guide

Iteration is one of the most fundamental operations in programming — visiting each item in a collection one at a time. Python's iterator protocol and generators build on this idea, but in a way that is both far more flexible and far more memory-efficient than simply looping over a list. Understanding these tools unlocks a style of programming that is elegant, lazy (in the best sense), and essential for working with large data, streams, and AI/ML pipelines.
are objects that produce values one at a time, on demand. Any object that implements two methods —
— is an iterator. When there are no more values to produce, it raises a
are a broader category: any object you can loop over (lists, tuples, strings, dicts, sets). When you write
to get an iterator, then repeatedly calls
are a special, simpler way to create iterators. Instead of writing a class with
, you write a function that uses the
keyword. Python handles all the bookkeeping automatically.

# A simple generator function
def count_up(start, end):
    current = start
    while current <= end:
        yield current
        current += 1

gen = count_up(1, 5)
print(next(gen))  # 1
print(next(gen))  # 2
, returns the value, and resumes from exactly that point on the next call to
The traditional approach to producing a sequence of values is to compute all of them, store them in a list, and return the list. This works fine for small data, but it breaks down fast:
A list of 10 million integers takes hundreds of megabytes of RAM.
A network stream, a live sensor feed, or an infinite mathematical sequence cannot be stored in a list at all.
Sometimes you only need the first few values — computing the rest wastes time and memory.
Generators solve all three problems by computing values
: one at a time, only when asked. They represent a
of computation rather than a bucket of results.
Processing large files or datasets line by line
Building data pipelines (especially in ML/AI preprocessing)
— Only one value exists in memory at a time, regardless of the total sequence length.
— Values are computed only when needed, which can dramatically speed up programs that stop early.
— Generators can be chained into pipelines, each transforming data from the previous stage.
— A generator function reads like normal code, much simpler than writing a full iterator class.
— You can represent sequences with no defined end, which is impossible with a list.
— Local variables in the generator function persist between
— Once a generator is exhausted, it cannot be rewound. You must create a new one to iterate again.
— You cannot index into a generator (
will fail). Values must be consumed in order.
— Because execution is suspended and resumed, stack traces can be less intuitive.
— For small sequences where all values will be consumed anyway, a list can be faster due to lower per-item overhead.
— Generators cannot be pickled and sent between processes without extra work.
loop is syntactic sugar over the iterator protocol. This is what Python does internally:
Any object can become iterable by implementing
Writing an Iterator Class (the manual way)
This works perfectly but requires significant boilerplate. Generators eliminate this entirely.
A function becomes a generator function the moment it contains a
execute the body — it returns a generator object. Execution only begins when you call

def simple_gen():
    print("Before first yield")
    yield 1
    print("Before second yield")
    yield 2
    print("After last yield")

g = simple_gen()
print(next(g))  # prints "Before first yield", returns 1
print(next(g))  # prints "Before second yield", returns 2
next(g)         # prints "After last yield", raises StopIteration
call resumes from right after the last
. The function's local variables, loop counters, and execution position are all preserved between calls.
Just as list comprehensions create lists, generator expressions create generators. The syntax is identical but uses parentheses instead of square brackets:

# List comprehension — computes all values immediately, stores them
squares_list = [x ** 2 for x in range(1_000_000)]  # ~8 MB in memory

# Generator expression — lazy, computes one value at a time
squares_gen = (x ** 2 for x in range(1_000_000))   # ~200 bytes in memory

# Both can be iterated the same way
for val in squares_gen:
    print(val)
Generator expressions are ideal as arguments to functions that consume iterables:

total = sum(x ** 2 for x in range(1000))       # no extra brackets needed
maximum = max(len(word) for word in word_list)
filtered = list(x for x in data if x > 0)
One of the most powerful patterns is chaining generators into a pipeline where each stage pulls from the previous one. No stage runs ahead; data flows through one item at a time.
The entire file is never loaded into memory. Each
pulls one line through every stage.
Generators are not just output machines. You can also
values back into a running generator using
. This turns the generator into a coroutine — a function that can both receive and produce values.

def accumulator():
    total = 0
    while True:
        value = yield total   # yield sends total out; receives new value via send()
        if value is None:
            break
        total += value

gen = accumulator()
next(gen)          # Prime the generator (advance to first yield) → 0
gen.send(10)       # total = 10 → returns 10
gen.send(25)       # total = 35 → returns 35
gen.send(5)        # total = 40 → returns 40
to end early. The value passed to

def gen_with_return():
    yield 1
    yield 2
    return "all done"  # StopIteration.value = "all done"

g = gen_with_return()
next(g)  # 1
next(g)  # 2
try:
    next(g)
except StopIteration as e:
    print(e.value)  # "all done"
lets a generator delegate to another iterable or generator, forwarding all values,

def inner():
    yield 'a'
    yield 'b'

def outer():
    yield 1
    yield from inner()   # delegates to inner generator
    yield 2

list(outer())  # [1, 'a', 'b', 2]
through the delegation chain, which is critical for building coroutine-based frameworks.
Python's standard library is packed with tools that work seamlessly with iterators.

import itertools

# Infinite counter
for i in itertools.count(start=10, step=2):
    if i > 20: break
    print(i)  # 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20

# Cycle through a sequence forever
for x in itertools.islice(itertools.cycle(['A', 'B', 'C']), 7):
    print(x)  # A B C A B C A

# Chain multiple iterables together
combined = itertools.chain([1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6])
list(combined)  # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

# Cartesian product
list(itertools.product('AB', repeat=2))
# [('A','A'), ('A','B'), ('B','A'), ('B','B')]

# Sliding windows (Python 3.10+)
list(itertools.pairwise([1, 2, 3, 4]))  # [(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)]

# Group consecutive elements by a key
data = [('a', 1), ('a', 2), ('b', 3)]
for key, group in itertools.groupby(data, key=lambda x: x[0]):
    print(key, list(group))

# Take only n items from any iterable
list(itertools.islice(range(1_000_000), 5))  # [0, 1, 2, 3, 4]

# enumerate — pairs each value with its index
for i, val in enumerate(['a', 'b', 'c'], start=1):
    print(i, val)  # 1 a, 2 b, 3 c

# zip — interleave multiple iterables
for a, b in zip([1, 2, 3], ['x', 'y', 'z']):
    print(a, b)

# zip_longest — zip with a fill value for unequal lengths (Python 3.10+)
from itertools import zip_longest
list(zip_longest([1, 2], ['a'], fillvalue=None))  # [(1,'a'),(2,None)]

# map and filter — lazy transformations
doubled = map(lambda x: x * 2, range(5))     # generator-like
evens   = filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, range(10))

# reversed — reverse any sequence (not arbitrary iterators)
for x in reversed([1, 2, 3]):
    print(x)
Working with large files, datasets, or database result sets
Representing infinite or very long sequences
You need to start processing before all data is available
Memory is constrained (embedded systems, serverless functions with small RAM limits)
You need random access by index
You will iterate over the same data multiple times
The sequence is small and performance is critical (list is slightly faster per-item)
You need to know the length upfront
You need to pass the data to code that expects a concrete sequence
Real-World Example: ML Data Preprocessing Pipeline
In machine learning, training data often comes as large text files that don't fit in RAM. A generator pipeline lets each batch be prepared on the fly:
This pipeline processes a multi-gigabyte file while using only kilobytes of RAM at any moment. Adding a new preprocessing step is as simple as inserting another generator function.
Consuming a generator inside a condition and missing it later

gen = (x for x in range(5))
if any(x > 3 for x in gen):   # This partially or fully consumes gen
    for item in gen:           # gen may already be exhausted
        print(item)
Confusing generator functions with regular functions

def my_gen():
    yield 1

result = my_gen()       # This is a generator object, NOT 1
print(result)           # <generator object my_gen at 0x...>
print(next(result))     # 1
Forgetting to prime a coroutine before sending

def coro():
    x = yield
    print(x)

c = coro()
c.send(10)   # TypeError: can't send non-None value to a just-started generator
# Fix:
next(c)      # prime first
c.send(10)   # now works
If a generator opens a file or database connection inside itself and is abandoned before exhaustion, the resource stays open. Always use
Using a generator where a reusable iterable is needed
If you pass a generator to code that iterates over it twice (e.g., some ML frameworks that compute dataset length then iterate), it will be empty on the second pass. Wrap with a class implementing
that creates a fresh generator each time.
Prefer generator expressions over list comprehensions
when the result is only consumed once and sequentially.
by chaining small, single-purpose generator functions rather than one large complex function.
instead of reinventing common iteration patterns (batching, grouping, cycling, etc.).
should have names that suggest streaming or sequences (e.g.,
— if a function returns a generator, note it in the docstring so callers know it's single-pass.

when abandoning them mid-way in long-running processes to trigger
For data pipelines at scale, generators compose to form a
pipeline. In a batch ML training job processing 100 GB of text, the difference between a list-based approach and a generator pipeline can be the difference between fitting in memory and crashing.
overhead than iterating over a plain list (due to coroutine frame switching), but this is negligible compared to the memory savings for large sequences.
(standard library) — Comprehensive set of iterator building blocks.
— Functional programming toolkit with lazy sequence operations;
returns an iterator of DataFrames for large file processing.
— Dataset libraries built around lazy, streaming iteration for ML workloads.
, enabling async iteration over I/O-bound streams.

# Async generator example
async def stream_api_results(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        for url in urls:
            async with session.get(url) as resp:
                yield await resp.json()

async for result in stream_api_results(url_list):
    process(result)
in Python 3 is a special object (not a generator) that supports
, indexing, and multiple passes while using constant memory — the best of both worlds for integer sequences. For arbitrary sequences, generators are the right tool.

— An infinite generator combined with code that has no termination condition will hang the program. Always pair infinite generators with
— A single generator object is not thread-safe. If multiple threads need to consume from the same source, use a
— Collecting too many generator items at once (e.g.,
) defeats the purpose. Keep consumers lazy wherever possible.
